healthy.title: Demo 1 — Healthy Propeller
healthy.description: Install a known-good, balanced propeller. Run the calibration sweep to establish the healthy baseline. All health indices should read green.

imbalance.title: Demo 2 — Propeller Imbalance
imbalance.description: Attach a small strip of tape (~0.5–1 g) to the tip of one propeller blade only. Alternatively, use a propeller with a slightly trimmed blade. The rotational imbalance will generate elevated vibration while current draw changes minimally.
physical_steps: Power off motor before attaching tape.
Attach tape to tip of ONE blade only.
Power on and run at 40–60% throttle.
imbalance.expected_condition: Rotational imbalance detected. Inspect propeller and hub before next flight.

missing_prop.title: Demo 3 — Missing / Loose Propeller
missing_prop.description: Remove the propeller completely. The motor will spin unloaded, drawing dramatically less current and producing very low vibration. This represents a missing propeller, severe thrust loss, or a stripped hub.
physical_steps: Power on at same throttle used during calibration.
missing_prop.expected_condition: Propulsion load anomaly — possible causes: missing propeller, loose hub, severe thrust loss.

reduced_cooling.title: Demo 4 — Reduced ESC Cooling
reduced_cooling.description: Temporarily block the airflow path around the ESC using a removable cardboard or foam barrier. DO NOT physically contact the motor or propeller. Maintain safe operating temperatures. Monitor closely and remove barrier immediately if ESC temp exceeds safe limits.
physical_steps: Run motor at 30–50% throttle until ESC temperature stabilizes.
Carefully place a cardboard barrier blocking airflow around ESC only (never near propeller arc).
Remove barrier immediately if ESC temp approaches 60°C.
reduced_cooling.expected_condition: Thermal behaviour abnormal. Possible causes: blocked cooling path, degraded thermal compound, ESC heatsink detachment.

high_drag.title: Demo 5 — Increased Aerodynamic Load
high_drag.description: Position a removable cardboard or foam drag plate in the propeller outflow stream to increase aerodynamic resistance. The plate must be placed well outside the propeller arc — never close enough to contact rotating blades.
physical_steps: Mount drag plate downstream of propeller outflow, minimum 20 cm from blade tips.
Observe PLI rise as current and power increase.
high_drag.expected_condition: Propulsion load elevated. Possible causes: high aerodynamic drag, strong headwind, mechanical resistance.
